preamble: The elision of [æ] is typically the result of extreme vowel reduction in unstressed syllables. In rapid speech, the vowel may centralize to a schwa [ə] and then disappear entirely, a process known as syncope.

examples.note: Syncope of the unstressed schwa; the stressed [æ] in the first syllable is retained. This example illustrates the general syncope process adjacent to ash, not ash deletion itself.
Loss of unstressed [ə] before sonorant; the [æ] in the stressed syllable is retained.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English. https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/accents-of-english/A9495FCF7CC13D1D11FADB3DE2F335F6
